Note (1): Includes services and experiences

I and many others have written extensively about the powerful network effects in the OTA space (more properties attract more customers, which in turn draw more property owners to list on the site, which then draws more customers, resulting in a virtuous cycle with wide competitive moats), as well as the operating leverage (the incremental cost to serve one additional customer is near-zero). As such, I see no reason to belabor either point other than to point out that these advantages have enabled Booking to generate substantially higher and expanding margins compared to its smaller competitors (orange line).

Booking continues to grow in the alternative accommodation space

Booking has continued to gain share in the alternative accommodations—the segment popularized by Airbnb. According to data provided by both companies, Booking’s share of alternative accommodation room nights (as a percentage of the combined sum of Booking and Airbnb’s room nights) has steadily increased to about 46% for the quarter ended June 2025 (highlighted yellow column).

Furthermore, Booking has been capturing more than half of the incremental room nights (as a percentage of the combined sum of Booking and Airbnb incremental room nights) (highlighted red column).

Two observations:

Airbnb room nights include both (1) its experiences and services, and (2) long-term stays, which is not an area of focus for Booking. As such, Booking’s share of the short-term alternative accommodation space is likely under-estimated.

Booking offers a choice of both traditional and alternative accommodations in mainland China—a fast growing market that Airbnb exited in June 2022. While the decision made sense for Airbnb at that time, it will be exceedingly difficult for the company to regain the trust and confidence of Chinese hosts who were left abandoned while the country was still recovering from the COVID-19 pandemic.

As search for Airbnb homes in Beijing returns the following:

Hedged against ban of short-term rentals

Many countries and cities around the world are suffering from over-tourism and a housing shortage, which governments place blame on Airbnb operators for taking housing supply away from local residents. Some cities, including Amsterdam, Paris, Berlin, Barcelona, Lisbon, Singapore, have implemented restrictions on short term alternative accommodations, ranging from tight regulations (limited number of days, host presence required) to outright bans. Unlike Airbnb, Booking can continue to offer customers traditional hotel accommodations in these markets.

Less reliance on the US market

According to a US Congressional Report entitled Recent Developments in International Travel to the United States, the Trump Administration's continued focus on immigration enforcement has led to concerns about a dampening effect on international tourist visits to the United States. The number of international visitors to the United States has dipped since February 2025, declining by as much as 7-8% in March and May this year compared to 2024, with tourists from Canada and South Korea showing the biggest decreases (-16.8% and -11.3%, respectively).

This is corroborated by Booking’s 2Q 2025 earnings report, which showed that US room nights grew most slowly amongst all its regions:

Source: Booking Q2 2025 earnings presentation

Even though Booking does not disclose the number of listed properties by geography, the strength in Booking’s room nights for the first two quarters (up 7% and 8% respectively) highlights that the company is well-diversified and not heavily dependent on the number of inbound US visitors.

The “Connected Trip” vision for the future

In 2019, Booking CEO Glenn Fogel laid out a vision of the “Connected Trip”, in which an AI-enabled virtual travel agent will help customers seamlessly plan and book all aspects of a fully personalized trip, including home to airport transportation, flights, car rentals, hotels, restaurants, tours, and tickets to local events & attractions. The virtual agent will also be able to take action to help handle exceptions, such as re-booking hotels in the event a customer misses connecting flights, or re-booking or suggesting alternate restaurants should the customer miss a dining reservation.

Some competing travel websites, such as TripAdvisor, have created generative AI tools to suggest ideas and itineraries; most are still in their infancy stages, and none have come close to what Booking has envisioned.

As a frequent traveler, I have found the trip planning to be a highly time consuming and tedious pain point. In addition to charting out my route through unfamiliar places in strange countries, I need to connect all my stops with the right modes of transportation (which is not always obvious), punctuate each stop with an accommodation that strikes a balance between location, safety, quality, & budget, and plan my accommodations that are within reasonable travel distance from the activities I wish to partake in.

When fully implemented, the Connected Trip vision will solve this pain point. It will transform Booking from a reservation website that offers customers individual components of a trip (e.g., flights, hotels, transportation, attractions) into an integrated one-stop-shop for travel needs. Not only will Booking capture more of a traveler’s budget, but it will also gain more bargaining power over its suppliers, help protect travelers from trip disruptions as they go through their journey, and create new sources of revenue and cost savings that we cannot even imagine today.

Even though the ultimate vision will take years to fulfill, management has reported progress in cross-selling flights and ground transportation in addition to accommodations. Personally, I have responded to Booking’s nudges, as I find it far more convenient to have my hotel and flight reservations in one place (particularly with airlines that I do not have frequent flier status with) so I do not fumble around with multiple itineraries in crowded airports.

Concerns

Competition

Google Gemini, which seamlessly accesses Google Maps, Google Reviews, and Google Flights, has been a surprisingly capable travel assistant. With proper prompting (e.g., “Plan me a 7-day drive from Bozeman, Montana, to Boise, Idaho. Where possible, route me through scenic areas and Lewis and Clark points of interest. Provide commentary on things to look out for. Limit daily drives to 4 hours. Suggest mid-range hotels at each stop that allow pets, and local restaurants and their recommended dishes”), Gemini can already generate a detailed itinerary that reasonably demanding travelers can use as a basis to make reservations and other arrangements.

Other travel websites or new startups could develop AI agents that can make reservations for customers, potentially leapfrogging Booking in its Connected Trip initiative. However, as the leading OTA, Booking’s large trove of customer data gives it a significant advantage to train its AI models and agents.

Furthermore, Booking’s reputation for dependability and superb execution creates stickiness, particularly for customers traveling to unfamiliar places.

Reduced travel due to recessions or another pandemic

We have been through sharp recessions and the COVID-19 pandemic in recent years, during which Booking’s stock price have experienced sharp drawdowns. However, I believe OTA industry leaders like Booking will likely gain share in tough economic conditions and emerge stronger as smaller OTAs and mom & pop travel agents that do not have the financial stability go out of business.

Valuation

With a PE of almost 38x, Booking has the highest valuation (orange line).

However, this is partially justified by the fact that its revenue has grown faster than both Airbnb and Expedia (purple and blue lines).

In addition, the company is highly cash generative, with a trailing twelve-month (TTM) free cash flow yield (after credit losses & chargebacks and stock-based compensation) of about 4.5%, which I believe is not an overly excessive valuation.

Summary

Booking continues to widen its lead over its competitors due to powerful network effects and operating leverage and is growing briskly by expanding its market share in the alternative accommodation segment.

Unlike some of its competitors, Booking is hedged against recent government crackdowns on alternative accommodations in some cities, and its strong international presence insulates it from short term changes in travel patterns.

If successful, Booking’s Connected Trip product has the potential to transform the travel planning landscape and capture more wallet share from more travelers.

At current levels, its valuation is fair but not cheap. I am holding onto my position and plan to add on pullbacks

data.title: Moderna Vs BioNTech: Who's Winning The Post-COVID Race
data.description:
And so, I've reached the publication of the sixth article, following "Pfizer vs. Johnson & Johnson: Who's the Better Bargain for Investors" and "Eli Lilly vs. Novo Nordisk: Which Is the Better Bargain for Investors," in a series of comparison analyses of two pharmaceutical stocks.

In today's article, I'll choose which of BioNTech (NASDAQ:BNTX) and Moderna (NASDAQ:MRNA) is more attractive after the end of the COVID-19 era, whose pipeline of product candidates is more promising in the fight against cancer, and which of these two is more 'protected' from the attacks of President Trump and RFK Jr. on the vaccine industry.

So, let's get started.

Current Valuation of BioNTech vs. Moderna

As in my last comparison article, "Teva vs. Viatris: Who Will Dominate in President Trump's America," below you will see a table with the values of each of the parameters by which I will compare the two companies.

Further, I'll present a detailed analysis of those that, according to my "ALLKA 4D Rating" model, carry the most weight in determining whether BioNTech or Moderna is more undervalued within the "Current Valuation" category.

So, I'll start by comparing how Moderna and BioNTech's revenues changed after the COVID-19 pandemic ended and Robert F Kennedy Jr. took over as Secretary of HHS on February 13, 2025, an event that, in my view, led to a more restrictive vaccine policy against this virus.

Next, I'll determine how often these two stocks beat Wall Street analysts' expectations and only then compare their price-to-sales [P/S] ratios over the past 12 months.

Moderna's revenue was $142 million in Q2 2025, up 31.5% quarter-on-quarter but down 41.1% year-on-year.

Consequently, its P/S ratio [TTM] is 3.45x, 12.74% lower than the sector median and 85.6% lower than its 5-year average.

If I look at how many times in the last 12 quarters Moderna has beaten the consensus revenue estimate, the number is 10.

With BioNTech, the situation is a little different, it seems to me, for the obvious reason that its partner in the Comirnaty franchise, namely Pfizer (PFE), has more experience and a marketing budget to minimize the current situation.

What am I talking about?

By this 'situation', I mean that after the completion of the stimulus programs promoted by the White House from 2021 to 2023, people are becoming less eager to get vaccinated with the mRNA COVID-19 vaccines.

So, BioNTech's revenue was not only bigger than Moderna's, reaching about $301.6 million for the three months ended June 30, 2025, but its growth rate was also higher, namely 113.9% year-on-year and 45.8% quarter-on-quarter.

Its P/S ratio [TTM] is 7.42x, which is 87.4% higher than the sector median.

Thus, I can only conclude that Moderna is significantly more undervalued by this metric than BioNTech.

How often has BioNTech beaten analysts' expectations?

It has beaten Wall Street's revenue estimates six times out of the last 12 quarters, four quarters fewer than Moderna.

Now I'll move on to discussing the balance sheets of both companies.

According to my "ALLKA 4D Rating" model, analyzing total debt, the total debt/EBITDA ratio, and the maturity dates of Moderna/BioNTech's notes is crucial, and I will explain why later.

Does BioNTech or Moderna have lower total debt and total debt/EBITDA ratio?

Moderna's total debt was $741 million at the end of Q2 2025, down $528 million over the past 12 months.

Meanwhile, its competitor's total debt in the vaccine market has fluctuated around $260.4 million over the past 10 quarters, reaching $317.6 million at the end of June.

Let's continue.

Since the operating income and EBITDA of Moderna and BioNTech are negative, I believe that instead of discussing their total debt/EBITDA ratios, it would be more appropriate to compare how much cash is on their balance sheets.

BioNTech's cash and short-term investments amount to about $16.5 billion, compared to Moderna's $5.1 billion.

Also, if I look at how it's declined year-on-year, BioNTech again emerges as the ‘winner’ [-$2 billion vs. -$3.36 billion in the case of Moderna].

Thus, I conclude that BioNTech has greater financial flexibility and, more importantly, its balance sheet allows it to be more aggressive in R&D.

I'm not even taking into account that it also has strong partnership agreements with Pfizer and, especially, Bristol-Myers Squibb (BMY) [up to $11.1 billion] .

I will discuss the deal between BioNTech and Bristol-Myers Squibb in the next section.

Since Moderna and BioNTech don't pay dividends, it's logical that I move on to the final question.

Who won in the "Current Valuation" category?

To answer this question, I created a heat map that shows the winner more clearly than a table.

As a result, BioNTech won in the "Current Valuation" category with 4 points, beating Moderna by 2 points.

Portfolio of drugs and product candidates of Moderna vs. BioNTech

In this category, my main goal is to determine whether Moderna or BioNTech has a better chance of catching up to the leaders in the vaccine and oncology treatment markets, such as Merck (MRK), Novartis (NVS), and Sanofi (SNY).

To choose the 'winner', I ask the first question.

Does BioNTech or Moderna have a more promising portfolio of approved vaccines?

Moderna's portfolio consists of Spikevax, mNEXSPIKE, an mRNA vaccine with one-fifth the dose of Spikevax, and mRESVIA, an RSV [respiratory syncytial virus] vaccine.

And it would seem that "more means better," but I believe it's not.

mRESVIA was first approved by the FDA on May 31, 2024, to protect adults over 60 years of age from lower respiratory tract illness caused by RSV, and its label was expanded on June 12, 2025, to also include those 18-59 years of age.

But when I opened Moderna's latest Form 10-Qs/10-K, I saw that its sales are not only negligible but almost zero.

I think the two key reasons for this current situation are people's distrust of mRNA vaccines after the 'COVID-19 era', and the fact that Pfizer's Abrysvo and GSK's Arexvy (GSK) remain the preferred choices of doctors.

Overall, sales of these two vaccines [$143 million vs. $88 million] are as follows.

Ultimately, I believe that weak sales of Moderna's three vaccines, as well as the lack of a partner like Pfizer that could share operating expenses, have hit its EBIT hard.

How hard?

Moderna's EBIT was -$907 million in Q2 2025, compared to -$514.4 million for BioNTech.

Who has a more promising portfolio of product candidates? Moderna or BioNTech?

In my opinion, the situation here is also ambiguous.

Moderna's portfolio is richer in experimental vaccines targeting a wide range of viruses, including HSV [mRNA-1608], Epstein-Barr virus [mRNA-1189 and mRNA-1195], and norovirus [mRNA-1403 and mRNA-1405].

But of course, its 'crown jewel' is mRNA-4157, an mRNA vaccine developed jointly with Merck to combat renal cell carcinoma, melanoma, bladder cancer, and non-small cell lung cancer.

While BioNTech is more focused on developing bispecific antibodies such as BNT327, BNT312 [in partnership with Genmab] (GMAB), and BNT314, as well as so-called ADCs [antibody-drug conjugates] against breast cancer and solid tumors.

So, I want to clarify here that BNT327 [BMS986545] is an anti-PD-L1/VEGF-A bispecific antibody.

On June 2, 2025, Bristol-Myers Squibb announced that it had entered into a deal with BioNTech to develop this product candidate, which, to my great surprise, is being developed for 20 indications as monotherapy and in combination with trastuzumab pamirtecan and BNT325.

BioNTech's R&D portfolio includes 50 projects, six of which are in Phase III. Moderna has 35 clinical-stage projects, seven of which are in pivotal clinical trials.

Who won in the "Approved Drugs and Pipeline Candidates" category?

Thus, BioNTech became the winner, scoring 7 points.

Technical analysis and Wall Street Ratings of Moderna stock vs. BioNTech stock

So, according to the parameter values that I have noted in the table below, Moderna is a more attractive stock if I take technical analysis into account only.

I want to start with a 1-M timeframe.

Moderna stock shows more signs of being 'oversold' than BioNTech stock.

So, the price of Moderna shares remains slightly above the first standard deviation band formed from the VWAP [-1σ], and its RSI [33.28] has been near the oversold zone since November 1, 2024.

While BioNTech shares remain near VWAP [$103.60], and according to my assessment, it is completing wave C as part of the "zigzag" corrective pattern. If I switch to the 1-W timeframe, I believe that its stock price may correct further until it reaches a strong support zone lying between $95-$95.70.

Does Moderna or BioNTech have a greater upside relative to the average price target?

Once again, I cannot do without the data provided by Seeking Alpha.

According to it, the average PT [price target] for BioNTech is $134.76, indicating a potential upside of about 31.73% from the current share price [$104.90]. Meanwhile, if I turn to Moderna, the PT for it is $44.30, implying an upside of 65.67%.

Who won in the "Technical Analysis and Wall Street Ratings" category?

Moderna won this category with 4 points, beating BioNTech by 3.5 points.

But I can't stop at technical analysis alone, so let's continue.

Long-term prospects of BioNTech vs. Moderna

What do I expect from Moderna in the long term?

I expect Moderna's non-GAAP earnings per share to grow to $3.20 in 2029.

This figure is slightly above the median of a broad range of Wall Street analyst expectations [from -$3.41 to $20.62] because I am more optimistic about mNEXSPIKE sales and also believe mRNA-4157 will be a 'blockbuster' in the treatment of melanoma.

What do I expect from BioNTech in the long term?

I estimate that BioNTech's non-GAAP EPS and revenue growth will be higher than Moderna's over the next five years, reaching $2.35 and $4.4 billion, respectively.

These figures are at the upper end of the analyst ranges [from -$7.75 to $3.42 and from $2.05 billion to $4.44 billion] due to my more optimistic expectations for Comirnaty sales, as well as the launches of BNT327 and trastuzumab pamirtecan.

Who won in the "Long-Term Prospects" category?

Moderna beat BioNTech by 7 points.

Takeaway

So, I've reached the final part of the article.

Who is the winner?

But before I answer this question, I want to note that I am starting to cover Moderna and BioNTech with a 'Buy' rating.

However, because BioNTech scored 0.7 points higher, according to my "ALLKA 4D Rating" model, it's a more promising stock than Moderna.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Did Pfizer Overpay For Yet Another Acquisition?
data.description:
Performance Assessment

Pfizer (NYSE:PFE) has underperformed the S&P500 since my last update on the stock:

Thesis

I like some of Pfizer's recent moves. However, it is not enough to make me a buyer of PFE stock just yet:

  1. Pfizer's purchase of Metsera seems to be done at a reasonable valuation.
  2. Pfizer has some relief from tariffs, but it comes at a hefty cost of pricing headwinds.
  3. PFE stock may look undervalued vs. peers, but a weak fundamentals outlook may justify that.
  4. The sellers look like they are losing speed, but there are still no signs of convincing buyers.

Pfizer's Purchase Of Metsera Seems To Be Done At A Reasonable Valuation

In my last article on Pfizer, I had anticipated that Pfizer may resort to M&A to get a foothold in the obesity management drug market following its failure of Danuglipron. I viewed such a move as risky due to Pfizer's track record of overpaying for M&A as it finds itself in a desperate situation to make up for loss of exclusivity (LOE) patent expirations for up to 40% of its revenue profile:

Discontinuation in the development of Pfizer's obesity management drug is a major setback, and substituting it with pricey M&A is a risk

- Hunting Alpha

Hence, it is no surprise that Pfizer is acquiring Metsera (MTSR), a clinical-stage biotechnology company (which means no commercial revenues yet) focused on obesity management drugs. The total transaction value for this deal that Pfizer is paying is around $7.6 billion. This includes an upfront value of $4.9 billion and contingent value rights worth around $2.7 billion associated with key drugs progressing through clinical trials. Here's an overview of the key drugs:

The main drug, MET-097i, is currently in Phase 2 and is expected to move on to Phase 3 trials.

Now, some pharmaceutical analysts have estimated peak sales potential of more than $5 billion for Metsera:

PFE’s planned acquisition of MTSR [Metsera] is set to add differentiated obesity drug candidates that we estimate could generate >$5B in combined peak sales potential

- Leerink Partners' Senior Managing Director and Senior Research Analyst David Risinger

But this annual revenue estimate is the best-case scenario. Ideally, this figure should be adjusted for the risks of failure during clinical trials. Here's how we can take that $5 billion number and arrive at a figure that is arguably more realistic:

This table below shows the chances of commercialization (called marketing authorization) based on various clinical trial stages. I highlighted the metabolism and endocrinology disciplines as obesity falls under these categories:

Assuming an equally weighted average of Phase II to commercialization success between the metabolism (25% chance) and endocrinology (15% chance) disciplines, we can get to an overall estimate of 20% success chance for Metsera's commercialization. But note how not all drugs are at Phase II. Hence, this 20% probability should be downgraded a bit. I'll assume 17%.

So applying 17% to $5 billion gets us $850 million for a risk-adjusted annual revenue estimate for Metsera. Now, we can also apply this 17% to the $2.7 billion mentioned earlier to get a risk-adjusted contingent value rights payout of $459 million. Add this to the $4.9 upfront consideration, and we get a risk-adjusted transaction consideration of about $5.4 billion.

Now, we have enough information to compute an implied EV/revenue transaction multiple; 5400/850 = 6.4x.

Now, for this kind of clinical-stage biopharmaceutical company involved in drug discovery at the $850 million revenue kind of scale, this implies a sectoral comparable transaction multiple of 8.5x:

As you can see, the Metsera deal's EV/revenues multiple of 6.4x is at a 25% discount to 8.5x. Of course, due to a 3-4 year time lag of these revenues kicking in, arguably this discount is actually narrower:

Subject to clinical and regulatory success, we anticipate Metsera's pipeline will deliver a series of launches beginning in the 2028-2029 time frame that would accelerate our growth trajectory

- Chief Strategy & Innovation Officer Andrew Simon Baum in the Metsera M&A call

But the broader point probably still applies; it looks like Pfizer has not grossly overpaid for Metsera after all. Hence their M&A entry into obesity management drugs may not be as risky as I had feared.

Pfizer Has Some Relief From Tariffs, But It Comes At A Hefty Cost Of Pricing Headwinds

It looks like in exchange for a 3-year exemption from tariff hits, Pfizer is not only committing $70 billion (amounting to 5.6 years of the TTM FCF run-rate of $12.4 billion), but also bucking to pressure from regulators to lower prices for various drugs due to programs such as the Inflation Reduction Act (IRA), Medicare Drug Price Negotiation Program (MDPN) and the Most-Favored-Nation (MFN) pricing policies. A lot of these headwinds are expected to kick in over the next 3 years. This risks section in the latest 10-Q is well worth a careful read:

In the U.S., we expect to see continued focus by the U.S. government and states on regulating drug pricing and access to medicine, including but not limited to, the potential for international reference pricing, including Most-Favored-Nation (MFN) drug pricing…

Eliqis was among the first ten medicines subject to the Medicare Drug Price Negotiation Program. In August 2024, the government released the new Medicare price for Eliquis, which, effective January 1, 2026, will be required to be offered to all Medicare beneficiaries and to covered entities participating in the 340B Program that dispense Eliquis to a Medicare beneficiary if that maximum fair price is lower than the discounted price such entities are offered under the 340B Program ceiling price calculation. The Eliquis Medicare price is factored into our long-term financial planning, in accordance with our standard financial reporting and forecasting protocols. On January 17, 2025, CMS announced the selection of another 15 drugs from Medicare Part D for the maximum fair price, with prices to be set and effective on January 1, 2027. Ibrance and Xtandi were included in the list of 15 drugs selected. Another 15 drugs from Medicare Part B or Medicare Part D will be selected by February 1, 2026, for the maximum price to be set and in effect by January 1, 2028. It is possible that more of our products could be selected in future years, which could, among other things, lead to lower revenues prior to expiry of intellectual property protections… The IRA also made significant changes to the Medicare Part D benefit design (IRA Medicare Part D Redesign), which took effect beginning in 2025 and are impacting our revenues. In 2025, we expect a favorable impact from the $2,000 annual out-of-pocket cap and new Prescription Payment Plan, more than offset by an expected unfavorable impact from the sunsetting of the Coverage Gap Discount Program and the addition of new manufacturer discounts in the initial and catastrophic coverage phases, which we anticipate will have a net unfavorable impact to revenue in 2025 of approximately $1 billion, year-over-year.

- Q2 FY25 10Q, Author's bolded highlights

Note that via the direct-to-consumer website TrumpRx, Pfizer is also taking pricing cuts on 4 prescription drugs to the tune of 40-85%.

Overall, this is likely to pressure gross profit margins ahead:

Now some bulls may point out that the benefit of $7.2 billion in net cost savings, which amounts to an 11.3% point boost to the margin profile:

On Track to Deliver Approximately $7.2 Billion in Overall Anticipated Net Cost Savings from Previously Announced Cost Improvement Initiatives(4) by End of 2027

- Q2 FY25 Press Release

But I would counter this point by drawing your attention to the 'unusual' charges that tend to be rather recurring. It's not unusual by any means:

Overall, these recurring annual charges have led to an average 12 percentage point margin hit. I argue that this would offset a lot of the margin benefits from cost saving efforts:

PFE Stock May Look Undervalued Vs. Peers, But A Weak Fundamentals Outlook May Justify That

PFE is trading at a 1-yr FWD P/E of 8.6x; a sizable 41% discount to the pharma peers' median multiple:

Note that this discount is steeper than the typical 22.6% discount that PFE has usually traded at vs. its peers:

So based on this, one may argue that PFE is relatively undervalued. I accept that maybe it is. But on the other hand, I wonder if the steeper discount is justified given the weaker fundamentals as seen in the downward to flattish trend in the earnings expectations index:

The Sellers Look Like They Are Losing Speed But There Are Still No Signs Of Convincing Buyers

If this is your first time reading a Hunting Alpha article using Technical Analysis, you may want to read this post, which explains how and why I read the charts the way I do. All my charts reflect total shareholder return, as they are adjusted for dividends/distributions.

Relative Read of PFE vs SPX500

On PFE vs. the S&P500 on the monthly charts, we see a continuation of a long-time underperformance downtrend since 2023. A point in favor of the bulls is that the momentum of the sellers is reducing. However, I would still insist that this is not a buy yet because there are no signs of convincing buyer sponsorship. Buying too early without these signs can lead to greater chances of dead money (low IRRs) tied up in this stock.

Takeaway & Positioning

After Pfizer failed to crack the obesity management market, I expected the company to resort to M&A to enter this market. I viewed this as a risk because I have noted that Pfizer tends to overpay for acquisitions, especially in the current arguably desperate context of needing to make up for lost revenues arising from patent expirations. But my analysis of the Metsera acquisition reveals that Pfizer may be accomplishing an M&A-based entry into the obesity drug market at a rather reasonable valuation.

On the costs and profitability side, I have concerns of pricing headwinds and 'unusual' charges that are far too frequent and recurring to be treated as one-off hits to the P&L. This paints a weak fundamental earnings outlook, which may justify PFE stock's valuation discount vs its other pharmaceutical peers.

And technically, vs. the broader market index, PFE's sellers look like they may be slowing down. However, I do not see any signs of strong buyers yet to mark a reversal of the overall downtrend.

Rating: 'Market Perform/Hold'

How To Interpret Hunting Alpha's Ratings:

Strong Outperform/Strong Buy: Expect the company to outperform the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis, with higher than usual confidence. I also have a net long position in the security in my personal portfolio.

Outperform/Buy: Expect the company to outperform the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis.

Market Perform/Hold: Expect the company to perform in line with the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis.

Underperform/Sell: Expect the company to underperform the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis.

Strong Underperform/Strong Sell: Expect the company to underperform the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis, with higher than usual confidence.

The typical time-horizon for my views is multiple quarters to more than a year. It is not set in stone. However, I will share updates on my changes in stance in a pinned comment to this article and may also publish a new article discussing the reasons for the change in view.

data.title: In Search Of Stability: 4 Top Consumer Staples Stocks
data.description:
Trade War-Fueled Volatility: From Greed to Fear

The market has swung wildly in a tug-of-war between sentiments of fear and greed, marked by the Cboe Volatility Index (VIX) spiking twice in seven days. AI-fueled enthusiasm led to record highs before the market was dragged down by rising trade tensions, a government shutdown, and anxiety over the possibility of another steep correction. As the US and China renewed their trade war, ignited by Beijing’s rare earth export restrictions, the S&P 500 saw its worst sell-off since the “Tariff Tantrum” correction in April.

Investors continued to seek shelter in safe haven assets, with gold extending its record rally and silver prices reaching their highest in decades. Traders also flocked to the safety of bonds, with the benchmark 10-year yield (US10Y) hitting its lowest level in seven months.

The surging volatility comes as high valuations are bringing back memories of the dotcom era’s irrational exuberance. At the end of Q2 2025, the S&P 500 P/E ratio stood at 28.3x, above five-year and 10-year averages. JPMorgan Chase CEO Jamie Dimon told the BBC he was more worried than others of a serious market downturn, driven by uncertainty, geopolitical risks, and fiscal spending. IMF chief Kristalina Georgieva observed global equity prices are surging on AI optimism - but so is global uncertainty. And while the global economy has proven resilient, overcoming the impact of tariffs, Georgieva expressed concerns over the possibility of a sudden market downturn:

"History tells us this sentiment can turn abruptly. Today’s valuations are heading toward levels we saw during the bullishness about the internet 25 years ago. If a sharp correction were to occur, tighter financial conditions could drag down world growth, expose vulnerabilities, and make life especially tough for developing countries."

At times of rising uncertainty and instability, stocks in defensive sectors - like consumer staples, utilities, and healthcare - have served as shelter from the storm. At the end of last week, investors temporarily turned to these defensive stocks as the S&P 500 slid, before the index bounced back when tensions eased and the Fed signaled it may halt quantitative tightening. So far this year, S&P 500 consumer staples stocks have been among the worst performing as investors pile into growth and cyclical plays amid AI hysteria and interest rate cuts.

S&P 500 Performance by Sector (as of 10/17/2025)

Amid stretched valuations in growth sectors and mounting macro uncertainty, investors may find it worthwhile to revisit the stability offered by staples. Due to their non-cyclical nature, consumer staple stocks were the highest performers during economic slowdowns and recessions from 1960-2019. Consumer staples can be characterized as needs vs. wants – they include essential products and services that consumers will continue to buy regardless of economic conditions.

Staples are also low-volatility stocks. The S&P 500 Consumer Staples Select Sector ETF (XLP) has a 60M beta of 0.54 and a 25M beta of 0.28 – indicating they're less volatile than any other sector. In addition, staples are trading at attractive valuations, presenting buy-the-dip opportunities along with positioning for the next sector rotation. But not all staples stocks have underperformed.

Top Consumer Staples Stocks For Stability

Seeking Alpha Quant identified four consumer staples stocks spanning the food and tobacco industries, showcasing strong collective investment fundamentals, excellent momentum, high growth, and attractive valuations. Defying the bearish trend in the staples sector, all four stocks are outperforming the market in the past year.

1. United Natural Foods, Inc. (UNFI)

  • Market Capitalization: $2.55B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Sector: Consumer Staples

  • Industry: Food Distributors

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 10/17/25): 2 out of 179

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 10/17/25): 1 out of 7

United Natural Foods is the No. 1 quant-rated food distributor stock, which has crushed the sector and market in the past year, driving an A+ Momentum Grade. A wholesaler with major clients like Whole Foods, UNFI believes it's well positioned to drive profitable growth within a growing $90B target addressable market that includes natural, organic, specialty, multicultural, and conventional grocery retailers, who can benefit from the company’s differentiated products, programs, and services. UNFI is executing on a refresh strategy and multi-year plan unveiled a year ago, focused on enhancing efficiencies and creating more value for customers and suppliers by leveraging automation, technology, and network optimization.

UNFI 1Y Price Performance vs. S&P 500

UNFI surged after beating Q4 and full-year earnings and expectations and boosting guidance, overcoming a $400M loss in sales from a cyberattack in June. UNFI annual net sales rose 2.6% to $31.8B in FY25, driven by 9% growth in the company’s Natural segment, larger volumes, and improved product mix. Adjusted EBITDA was up 6.6% to $518M for the fiscal year. UNFI has delivered robust growth in operating profit in the past 12 months, while levered free cash flow surged over 1,000%, boosted by network optimization and the consolidation of volumes from four distribution centers. UNFI deployed lean daily management in 28 of 52 distribution centers, which has strengthened performance across quality, delivery, and cost metrics.

Wall Street sell-side analysts have high-growth expectations, with the forward EPS long-term growth rate at 138%. UNFI has nine upward earnings revisions in the past 90 days, and EPS is expected to surge more than 180% in FY25, according to consensus estimates, and more than 20% in each of the following two years. The outstanding performance and high growth expectations have driven an A+ Growth Grade, and despite this hot momentum, the stock still trades at a value. UNFI’s PEG FWD of 0.15 represents a 94% discount to the sector for an A+ Valuation Grade. UNFI’s exceptional momentum, ability to overcome challenges to deliver growth, and attractive price make it a top Strong Buy in the staples sector.

2. Tesco PLC (OTCPK:TSCDY)

  • Market Capitalization: $38.52B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Sector: Consumer Staples

  • Industry: Food Retail

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 10/17/25): 3 out of 179

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 10/17/25): 1 out of 17

The No. 1 quant-rated food retail stock, grocery giant Tesco has a solid position in its two biggest markets with a 28% share in the UK and 23% in Ireland. Tesco’s market share has gradually expanded over the past three years due to brand perception, price competitiveness, and improved quality and service. Tesco has handily outperformed the market and the sector in price performance over the past 12 months and in total return over the past five years.

In H125, Tesco revenue grew 5.1% to £33.05B and adjusted operating profit by 1.6% to £1.67B. Tesco Finest premium brand grew sales by 16%, while online revenue rose 11% to £3.7B. Tesco launched 470 new products and opened 38 stores in the first half of 2025, including 32 in the United Kingdom, three in Ireland, and three in Central Europe. Tesco's competitive pricing programs – Aldi Price Match, Low Everyday Prices, and Clubcard – were key drivers of volume growth and market share gains. The extension of internal cost reduction and efficiency initiatives offset operating cost inflation, including increased regulatory costs, to maintain margins. Tesco raised full-year operating profit guidance to between £2.9B and £3.1B, citing better-than-expected customer responses to pricing programs, cost control, and good weather.

Tesco’s EPS long-term forward growth rate is at 10.55%, while EPS is expected to grow by 6.9% in FY26 and 10.29% in FY27. Tesco’s P/E is around the sector median, but the overall valuation framework is appealing based on attractive PEG FWD and EV/EBITDA multiples. Tesco offers a dividend yielding 3.22%, with a 4.29% five-year growth rate, awarded semiannually. Tesco has an expanding foothold in the grocery retail market in the United Kingdom and Ireland, building momentum and potentially another food industry stock to add to your portfolio that provides geographic diversification.

3. Imperial Brands PLC (OTCQX:IMBBY)

  • Market Capitalization: $31.95B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Sector: Consumer Staples

  • Industry: Tobacco

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 10/17/25): 4 out of 179

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 10/17/25): 1 out of 9

The top quant-rated tobacco stock, Imperial Brands registered its best single-session gain of the year earlier in October, after announcing a £1.45B share buyback plan and confirming it was on track to achieve annual targets for FY25 (ending on Sept. 30). According to Morgan Stanley, the update from Imperial Brands will be well received on Wall Street considering broader weakness elsewhere in staples. Imperial Brands and large-cap peers Philip Morris International Inc. (PM), British American Tobacco p.l.c. (BTI) and Altria Group, Inc. (MO), have shown excellent momentum in the past 12 months, defying the sector trend.

Imperial Brands 1Y Price Return vs. Industry Peers

The tobacco giant is committed to gaining market share in next generation products (NGP) - such as vapes, heated tobacco, and nicotine gum - which amounted to only £160M in adjusted net revenue in H125. Imperial Brands is aiming to grow NGP by double digits in the next five years. NGP net revenue in H125 grew 15% and is expected to come in at +12-14% for the fiscal year. Net revenue growth in both segments in the second half of the year was underpinned by strong pricing – offsetting lower volumes. Imperial expects consolidated net revenue growth in the low single digits and adjusted EPS growth to be in the high single digits. IMBBY's robust EBITDA, net income, and levered free cash flow margins are driving an ‘A’ Profitability Grade.

Imperial Brands is trading at a mere 9x earnings vs. the sector’s 15x, and a solid dividend yield is supporting a B+ Valuation Grade. Imperial Brands has a dividend yielding 6.2% with excellent growth and 27 consecutive years of payments. IMBBY’s poor Dividend Safety Grade is the result of high leverage, although the company has solid cash payout and coverage ratios and is expecting adjusted net debt to EBITDA at the lower end of 2.0–2.5x for FY25. Imperial Brands has had a Strong Buy or Buy Quant Rating since November 2024 and is crushing the staples sector in profitability and momentum, while trading at a significant discount.

4. Sendas Distribuidora S.A. (OTC:ASAIY)

  • Market Capitalization: $2.07B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Sector: Consumer Staples

  • Industry: Consumer Staples Merchandise Retail

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 10/17/25): 6 out of 179

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 10/17/25): 1 out of 10

Sendas Distribuidora S.A., under the brand Assai Atacadista, operates one of Brazil’s largest grocery and wholesale chains and is the No. 1 quant-rated stock in the consumer staples merchandise retail industry. Assai crushed the sector and market in the past year, has posted excellent revenue and earnings growth in the past three years, and is still trading at an attractive valuation. Despite rising interest rates in Brazil, Assai expanded its national footprint from 169 to 302 stores over the past five years, while doubling revenue to $14B.

In Q225, Assai delivered strong growth in revenue, EBITDA, and net income, driven by the maturation of new stores, cost controls, and deeper engagement with its Meu Assai App, which has attracted 14M registered users. Operational efficiencies and expansion of services offered in stores helped Assai maintain gross margins and boost net income in the face of high interest rates. Some key operational results include:

  • Same-store sales growth of 4.6%, supported by a 1.8% increase in customer traffic.

  • Ten new stores opened in the past 12 months, expanding total sales area by 3.1%.

  • Meu Assai App engagement surged – with the identified revenue accounting for 44% of total sales vs. 42% in Q125.

This exceptional revenue and explosive earnings growth in the past year have driven an A- Growth Grade, while prospects are high, with EPS long-term forward growth rate at 33%. Wall Street analysts expect FY25 earnings to grow by 15% and revenue by 11% to $14.42B. The stock is trading at a significant discount to the sector based on attractive P/E, EV/EBITDA, price-to-sales, and price-to-cash flow ratios.

Assai is a great stock to round out our list of top consumer staples picks, which have defied sector bearishness, showcase strong earnings growth upside, and offer shelter in a volatile market.

Best Staple Stocks For Stability

The S&P 500 has undergone high volatility in the past week, with sentiment swinging from greed to fear, fueled by trade war tensions, a government shutdown, and rising uncertainty. During the slide, investors shunned equities and flocked to safe haven assets like silver, gold, and bonds. Rising volatility surfaced as high valuations reminded investors of dotcom exuberance. JPMorgan Chase CEO Jamie Dimon and IMF chief Kristalina Georgieva both expressed concerns about a significant market correction. Although consumer staples stocks are among the worst performing sectors this year, they can offer investors shelter in a storm due to their non-cyclical nature, low volatility, solid valuations, and, in some cases, steady income. SA Quant found four staples stocks that offer the best of multiple worlds: high momentum, high growth, and stability.

We have many stocks with strong buy recommendations, and you can filter them using stock screens to suit your specific investment objectives. Consider using Seeking Alpha's 'Ratings Screener' tool to help find stocks that achieve diversification into the desired sectors you like. Alternatively, Alpha Picks offers a more efficient approach to smarter investing, leveraging Seeking Alpha's powerful quantitative stock-picking system to deliver two data-driven stock picks each month, helping investors save time and focus on high-quality opportunities.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
